metric_value: SARS is a very real problem, but at the moment it seems unlikely to untrack either the global electronics industry, or the U.S. economy

A month ago Aberdeen was warning high-tech firms about transportation breakdown disrupting electronic-parts supply chain — many shipments are carried on passenger jets and Singapore Air, JAL, Cathay, Korean and US/EU carriers have all cut flights
The likelihood of some sort of a transportation breakdown has been reduced
The People's Republic of China is the world's second largest consumer of PCs, and it's almost certainly headed for a recession because of the SARS epidemic
notes: Kastner — 'falling off a cliff in a major recession'

metric_value: Projects for which the presence of nonresident personnel are required (sales reps, product applications engineers, semiconductor capital equipment specialists) will slip schedules, and equipment or process failures requiring similar human resources to fix will go unrepaired until the epidemic is past
notes: Kastner verbatim on travel-ban project impact

metric_value: Sun Microsystems canceled an April 7th product launch to have been held in Shanghai
Intel called off Intel Developer Forums scheduled for Taiwan and Beijing in April
Most have already instituted logistics war room planning exercises — with frequent reports to the CEO. The planning consensus includes rapidly qualifying second and third supply sources outside Asia.
notes: Kastner — direct precursor of 2020 multi-sourcing playbook

metric_value: $50 billion a year in foreign investment is a real stimulus to the Chinese economy. That money could go away overnight, if the government were shown to be untrustworthy. Kicking and screaming the PRC government is being dragged into the 21st century global economy.
notes: Mixed track record long-term given COVID-19 origin opacity
